preFix.title: Pyrography lettering
preFix.subtitle: Burning clean letters onto wood using a writing tip
preFix.excerpt: How to burn consistent lettering onto wood: transferring a layout, pulling the writing tip at a steady pace, managing curves and corners, and correcting uneven lines. Uses a solid-tip burner with a writing tip on smooth-grained wood.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for pyrography technique. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on poker-work and pyrography as domestic crafts.

content.text: The burning tip reaches temperatures that will cause an instant burn on skin contact. Never touch the tip directly. Rest the unit on the cooling stand between strokes. Allow the tip to cool fully before changing attachments.
is controlled by three things: tip temperature, movement speed, and contact pressure. A
pulled quickly produces a light line; the same tip moved slowly burns deeper and darker. Consistent lettering comes from a consistent pull speed, not from varying the pressure.

Print or draw the lettering at the correct size on paper. Tape it over the wood surface. Slide a piece of graphite transfer paper (or rub pencil graphite over the back of the print) between the paper and the wood. Trace each letter firmly with a ballpoint pen. Lift the paper and check the transfer before removing the tape; missing lines are easy to re-trace.

Before burning the work piece, always test the temperature and pull speed on an offcut of the same wood. Set the burner to a medium temperature. Pull the tip across the offcut at a comfortable drawing speed and note the resulting line colour and width. For lettering, a medium-brown line at a controlled pull speed is the target. Adjust temperature up or down until the test stroke matches the target.

Pull the writing tip along each letter stroke toward you where possible, in the same direction you would draw or write the letter. Maintain a consistent pace throughout each stroke; slowing down at the end of a stroke will produce a darker spot at the end. Lift the tip cleanly at the end of each stroke; dragging it sideways leaves an unintended tail.

Burn curved letter strokes in one continuous pull if possible, rotating the work piece so that the curve always pulls toward you. For sharp corners, lift the tip, reposition, and start a new stroke rather than turning the tip mid-burn. Two strokes that meet cleanly at a corner are less noticeable than a single stroke that changes direction awkwardly.
Dark spots at stroke ends: caused by pausing before lifting the tip. Lift the tip in one smooth motion without slowing down.
Varying line width on straight strokes: caused by inconsistent contact pressure. Rest the hand on the work surface and let the tip rest on the wood without pressing; the weight of the tip is enough.

Carbon build-up on the tip causing a scratching sound: the tip needs cleaning. Allow the tip to cool on the stand, then rub it gently on a piece of brown paper to remove the residue before continuing.

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
brand-trademark: brand name "Target" — generic equivalent is "(any general-purpose shop)"
